questions.text: Which of the following is not a described type of virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: DNA viruses have either a single-stranded DNA genome or a double-stranded DNA genome.
DNA viruses have either a single-stranded DNA genome or a double-stranded DNA genome.
RNA viruses have either a single stranded RNA genome or a double stranded RNA genome.

options.text: Virus containing single strand RNA and single strand DNA
options.explanation: Viruses can be classified into two groups based on their genetic material: DNA viruses and RNA viruses. DNA viruses have a double-stranded DNA genome, while RNA viruses have either a single-stranded RNA genome or a double-stranded RNA genome. There is no known virus that has both a single-stranded RNA genome and a single-stranded DNA genome.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct as: Viruses can be classified into two groups based on their genetic material: DNA viruses and RNA viruses. DNA viruses have a double-stranded DNA genome, while RNA viruses have either a single-stranded RNA genome or a double-stranded RNA genome. There is no known virus that has both a single-stranded RNA genome and a single-stranded DNA genome.

questions.text: Icosahedral viruses have how many faces?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The capsomeres are arranged in 20 equilateral triangles in icosahedral viruses.
The capsomeres are arranged in 20 equilateral triangles in icosahedral viruses.
The capsomeres are arranged in 20 equilateral triangles in icosahedral viruses.
The capsomeres are arranged in 20 equilateral triangles in icosahedral viruses.

questions.explanation: Fact: The capsomeres are arranged in 20 equilateral triangles in icosahedral viruses.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true about the viruses?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses are not free-living organisms. They can only survive and reproduce inside living cells.
Viruses are obligate parasites. This means that they can only survive and reproduce inside living cells. They are not able to exist on their own outside of a host cell.
Viruses are obligate parasites and not free living.

Option B is correct. Viruses are obligate parasites. This means that they can only survive and reproduce inside living cells. They are not able to exist on their own outside of a host cell (free-living).

questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Viruses are obligate parasites. This means that they can only survive and reproduce inside living cells. They are not able to exist on their own outside of a host cell (free-living).

questions.text: For the synthesis of their proteins, a virus uses:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses lack the cellular structures and metabolic machinery required for independent metabolism. They are obligate intracellular parasites and must utilize the host cell's metabolism for their replication.

While viruses affect the metabolism of the entire host organism during infection, the actual synthesis of viral proteins occurs within the infected host cells. Viruses manipulate the cellular metabolism of the host cells for their own replication.

Viruses do not have their own cellular machinery for protein synthesis. Instead, they rely on the metabolic processes of the host cell they infect to replicate and produce viral proteins. Once inside a host cell, viruses hijack the cellular machinery, including ribosomes and enzymes, to carry out the synthesis of viral proteins and replicate their genetic material.
This option is not accurate, as viruses indeed rely on the metabolism of the host cell for protein synthesis and other essential processes during their replication cycle.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Metabolism of its host cell”Explanation: Viruses are acellular entities having no cellular/metabolic machinery. Thus they have to rely upon the biosynthetic machinery of host for this purpose.

questions.text: Following are the means of transmission of AIDS, EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV can be transmitted through unprotected sexual intercourse with an infected partner and from mother to child during breastfeeding.

options.text: Use of common syringes and surgical instruments
options.explanation: Sharing needles or syringes contaminated with infected blood can transmit HIV.
Before proper screening measures were put in place, there was a risk of transmitting HIV through blood transfusions using infected blood.

AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) is caused by the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV). The virus attacks the immune system and weakens it, making the body susceptible to various infections and diseases. However, the virus itself is not a means of transmission, it is the cause.

questions.explanation: AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) is caused by the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV). The virus attacks the immune system and weakens it, making the body susceptible to various infections and diseases. However, the virus itself is not a means of transmission, it is the cause.

questions.text: Capsid, the protective coat of a virus is made up of subunits known as capsomeres that are _ in nature.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is incorrect. Capsomeres are protein in nature that makes up the capsid.
It is incorrect. Capsomeres are protein in nature that makes up the capsid. RNA is a type of viral genome.
It is correct. Capsomeres are protein in nature that makes up the capsid.
It is incorrect. Capsomeres are protein in nature that makes up the capsid. DNA is type of viral genome.

questions.explanation: Capsomeres are protein in nature that makes up the capsid. While DNA and RNA are types of viral genome.

questions.text: Attachment of HIV DNA with host DNA is done via action of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Integrase is the enzyme responsible for the attachment of HIV DNA with host DNA. After reverse transcription in the cytoplasm, the viral DNA is integrated into the host cell's genome by integrase in the nucleus.
Reverse transcriptase is not directly involved in the attachment of HIV DNA with host DNA. It plays a role in the synthesis of viral DNA from the viral RNA template.
Protease is responsible for cleaving viral polyproteins into functional proteins during the late stages of the HIV life cycle. It is not involved in the attachment of HIV DNA to host DNA.
Nuclease is a general term for enzymes that cleave nucleic acids. In the context of HIV integration, integrase is the specific enzyme responsible for integrating viral DNA into host DNA.

questions.explanation: HIV integeration is the insertion of HIV genetic material into the genome of the infected cell. This process is completed with the help of integrase.

questions.text: The major cell infected by HIV is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is a general term that refers to white blood cells, which include various types like neutrophils, eosinophils, and basophils. While some white blood cells may be infected by HIV, the most significant target is the Helper T-lymphocyte. Hence, this option is incorrect.

HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) primarily infects and targets specific immune cells called Helper T-lymphocytes, also known as CD4+ T cells. These cells play a critical role in coordinating the immune response by activating other immune cells, such as B-lymphocytes and cytotoxic T cells.

Monocytes are another type of white blood cell that can be infected by HIV, but they are not the major target. Instead, the virus primarily targets and affects Helper T-lymphocytes. Hence, this option is incorrect.

B cells are responsible for producing antibodies and are crucial for humoral immune responses. While they can be indirectly affected by the decrease in Helper T-lymphocytes caused by HIV infection, they are not the primary cell targeted by the virus. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) primarily infects and targets specific immune cells called Helper T-lymphocytes, also known as CD4+ T cells. These cells play a critical role in coordinating the immune response by activating other immune cells, such as B-lymphocytes and cytotoxic T cells.

questions.text: Reverse transcriptase is used to make DNA copies of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Reverse transcriptase does not naturally make DNA copies of host RNA. Its primary function is to convert viral RNA into DNA. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Reverse transcriptase does not create DNA copies of host DNA. It's involved in the reverse transcription of RNA into DNA, not the replication of DNA. Hence, this option is incorrect.

This enzyme is commonly found in retroviruses, such as HIV, which have RNA genomes. Reverse transcriptase catalyzes the reverse transcription process, where RNA is used as a template to synthesize a complementary DNA strand, forming a DNA-RNA hybrid molecule. This DNA copy is then further extended to create a double-stranded DNA molecule that can be integrated into the host cell's genome.
While reverse transcriptase is involved in the formation of viral DNA from viral RNA, it is not used to make DNA copies of existing viral DNA. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: This enzyme is commonly found in retroviruses, such as HIV, which have RNA genomes. Reverse transcriptase catalyzes the reverse transcription process, where RNA is used as a template to synthesize a complementary DNA strand, forming a DNA-RNA hybrid molecule. This DNA copy is then further extended to create a double-stranded DNA molecule that can be integrated into the host cell's genome.

questions.text: According to lwanowski what are soluble living germs?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Soluble living germs are microorganisms that can dissolve in a liquid medium. They can be either bacteria or viruses. Bacteria: Bacteria are single-celled organisms that can be found in various environments, including soil, water, and the human body.

questions.text: The complete, mature and infectious particle is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A primary function of the capsid is to protect the viral genome from environmental conditions and ultimately to deliver the genome to the interior of a homologous host cell.

The virion, that is the complete infectious virus particle, includes a genome comprising one or a few molecules of either DNA or RNA, surrounded by a morphologically defined protein coat, the capsid; the capsid and the enclosed nucleic acid together constitute the nucleocapsid.

Bacteriophages, also known as phages, are viruses that infect and replicate only in bacterial cells. They are ubiquitous in the environment and are recognized as the most abundant biological agent on earth. They are extremely diverse in size, morphology, and genomic organization [1][2][3].

A nucleus, as related to genomics, is the membrane-enclosed organelle within a cell that contains the chromosomes. An array of holes, or pores, in the nuclear membrane allows for the selective passage of certain molecules (such as proteins and nucleic acids) into and out of the nucleus.

questions.explanation: The virion, that is the complete infectious virus particle, includes a genome comprising one or a few molecules of either DNA or RNA, surrounded by a morphologically defined protein coat, the capsid; the capsid and the enclosed nucleic acid together constitute the nucleocapsid.

questions.text: Virus that affects genital organs:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HCV primarily affects the liver and is transmitted through blood. It is not known for causing direct genital organ symptoms.
HSV (Herpes simplex virus) particularly HSV-2, is a virus that commonly affects the genital organs. It causes genital herpes, characterized by painful sores or blisters in the genital and anal areas.
While HIV can affect various parts of the body, including genital organs, it primarily targets the immune system. It is not classically considered a virus that causes direct genital lesions.
Various poxviruses, such as molluscum contagiosum virus, can cause lesions on the genital skin. However, they are not as commonly associated with genital infections as HSV.

questions.explanation: Different viruses attack different parts of human body such as hepatitis virus affects liver and polio virus affects nervous system. There are two types of herpes simplex viruses. HSV-1 affects lips and mouth parts and is called oral herpes. HSV-2 damages genital organs and is called as genital herpes.

questions.text: Which of the following pair of diseases is viral?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Both Rabies and Measles are viral diseases. Rabies is caused by the rabies virus, while Measles is caused by the measles virus.
Cholera is caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae, and Tuberculosis is caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
Typhoid fever is caused by the bacterium Salmonella Typhi, and Tetanus is caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani.
AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) is caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), which is a virus. Syphilis, on the other hand, is caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum.

questions.explanation: Cholera, typhoid, tuberculosis, syphilis and tetanus are bacterial diseases.

questions.text: Which of the following diseases is caused by a viroid?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: AIDS is caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), which is a retrovirus, not a viroid.
Hepatitis B is caused by the Hepatitis B virus, which is a DNA virus. It is not caused by a viroid.
Hepatitis C is caused by the Hepatitis C virus, which is an RNA virus. It is not caused by a viroid.
Hepatitis D is caused by the Hepatitis D virus, which is a defective RNA virus that requires the presence of Hepatitis B virus for its replication. It is not caused by a viroid.

questions.explanation: The only known human disease caused by a viroid is hepatitis D.

questions.text: The structure of phage _ is more complex than _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. The head is usually simpler in structure compared to the tail.

The structure of the tail of a phage is generally more complex than the structure of the head. The tail is involved in recognizing and attaching to the host bacterium, facilitating the injection of genetic material into the host cell. In contrast, the head typically contains the genetic material (DNA or RNA) and is responsible for protecting and delivering it to the host cell.
Incorrect. While both the collar and base plate are components of the tail, the overall complexity of the tail structure is more significant.
Incorrect. The base plate is a component of the tail, and the tail is generally more complex than the head.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Tail, Head”Explanation: Tail contains two types of proteins whereas head contains only one type of protein.

questions.text: It refers to the final changes within an immature virion that result is an virus particle
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Assembly: After de novo synthesis of viral genome and proteins, which can be post-transrciptionally modified, viral proteins are packaged with newly replicated viral genome into new virions that are ready for release from the host cell. This process can also be referred to as maturation.

There are all sorts of virus shapes and sizes. However, all virus particles have a protein coat that surrounds and protects a nucleic acid genome. This protein coat is called a capsid, and the instructions for making the protein subunits of the capsid are encoded in the nucleic acid genome of the virus.

Integration is an obligatory replication step for all retroviruses. The process begins after the virus enters the cell and the RNA genome is reverse transcribed into a double-stranded DNA. The direct precursor to integration is the linear form of viral DNA (4, 64).

Virus maturation corresponds to a transition from an initial, non-infectious, assembly product to an infectious virion. This process is observed in virtually all well-studied animal and bacterial viruses.

questions.explanation: Assembly: After de novo synthesis of viral genome and proteins, which can be post-transrciptionally modified, viral proteins are packaged with newly replicated viral genome into new virions that are ready for release from the host cell. This process can also be referred to as maturation.

questions.text: Retroviruses are characterized by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Retroviruses (oncoviruses), are spherical in form, about 100 nm in diameter and enveloped by host plasma membrane.
Retroviruses possess capsid, hence this is wrong option. Capsid is made up of protein subunits known as capsomeres. The number of capsomeres is characteristics of a particular virus.
Retrovirus is a unique group of RNA viruses that have the ability to reverse transcribe their RNA genome into DNA, that's how they are characterized.
Retrovirus is a unique group of RNA viruses and hence do not have DNA.

questions.explanation: Retrovirus is a unique group of RNA viruses that have the ability to reverse transcribe their RNA genome into DNA, that's how they are characterized.

options.explanation: This is the wrong option as it is more than the actual.
200 is the amount of bacteriophages produced after 25 minutes of initial infection.
This is the wrong option as it is less than the actual.
